Estimates of the xenon poisoning4 of the 60-Mw Reflector-Moderated Reactor (power density 1.35 kw/cc) indicate that unless the xenon is rather rapidly purged from the fuel during operation a reactivity loss of the order of a few per cent will occur.
Also, the maximum xenon concentration after shutdown would give a reactivity loss of about $10\%$ or more.

The antidamped oscillations cannot occur if the frequency of the mechanical oscillations is very much higher than the frequency of the nuclear-power oscillations, as will be the case in the 60-Mw CFRE.

At a core radius of $30\mathrm{cm}$ the critical mass is virtually independent of fuel thickness.

The effects here are not large; an increase in core radius from 20 to $60~\mathrm{cm}$ at a constant fuel thickness gives a decrease in the ratio of about $20\%$ , while an increase in the fuel thickness from 5 to $20~\mathrm{cm}$ at a constant core radius results in an increase in the ratio of about $33\%$ .
The least-thermal reactor (28%) has the thickest fuel layer and the smallest core, while the most-thermal reactor (45%) has the thinnest fuel layer and the largest core.

When the 60-Mw design reactor<sup>7</sup> was modified by deleting the Inconel tubes and their surrounding stagnant sodium and fitting the resulting voids with beryllium, the critical mass decreased by $29\%$ , that is, from an original value of 40.7 lb to 29.0 lb.
This percentage decrease was duplicated by $\mathrm{NDA}^9$ in a calculation on a 300-Mw reflector-moderated reactor for which the same method of poison reduction was employed.

text: The large-scale (250-lb-capacity) equipment for the production of fluoride mixtures has been reactivated and is now in three-shift five-day operation for furnishing the $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ -based materials required for large-scale testing at ORNL and other installations.
The incongruent melting point of the compound $\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{Zr}_4\mathrm{F}_{19}$ was established as $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ by quenching mixtures with 57 and 60 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ .
The liquidus temperatures of mixtures with 57 and 60 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ were found to be $546\pm 5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $609\pm 5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively.
The 57 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ composition (almost $100\%$ $\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{Zr}_4\mathrm{F}_{19}$ ) was converted to $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ and liquid at $538^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

From the shape of the liquidus curve and from x-ray diffraction studies of large samples of slowly cooled mixtures, it appears that the $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ primary-phase field extends to about 56 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ in the $\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4}$ system.

Quenching and glass-devitrification experiments indicate that the liquidus-solidus temperature of the 47 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF_4}$ composition is about $522^{\circ}C,$ and the liquidus-solidus temperature of the 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF_4}$ composition is about $511^{\circ}C.$ Recently, a very careful thermal-analysis comparison of the 47 and 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF_4}$ mixtures showed that the 47 mole $\%$ mixture melts and freezes sharply at $520^{\circ}C$ and the 50 mole $\%$ mixture freezes sharply at $510^{\circ}C.$ These results mean that the single phase which is always obtained on cooling 47 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF_4}$ compositions is a congruently melting compound $(\mathsf{Na}_{9}\mathsf{Zr}_{8}\mathsf{F}_{41})$ while the 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF_4}$ composition is near a eutectic of $\mathsf{Na}_{3}\mathsf{Zr}_{4}\mathsf{F}_{19}$ and $\mathsf{Na}_{9}\mathsf{Zr}_{8}\mathsf{F}_{41}$ .
A phase referred to as R-3 has appeared in association with glass in compositions ranging
from 45 to 57 mole $\%$ ZrF.
Experiments with very small samples at various temperatures have failed to produce R-3.

It was concluded from these experiments that R-3 does not have a stable existence in the binary system but is an easily crystallized metastable compound (probably NaZrF) that is produced by fairly rapid cooling below the equilibrium liquidus temperature.

Petrographic and x-ray diffraction examination of slowly cooled preparations led to the postulation of the existence of a weakly birefringent compound $\mathsf{Na}_3\mathsf{Zr}_2\mathsf{F}_{11}$ and a series of solid solutions between it and $\mathsf{Na}_9\mathsf{Zr}_8\mathsf{F}_{41}$ .

Petrographic examinations of samples of 43 and 45 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ compositions heated to $483^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ and held for 20 hr, before quenching, definitely pointed to an immiscibility gap in this series between about 42 and 44 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ .

Petrographic examinations of a large number of quenched samples with between 39 and 45 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ led to the conclusion that $\mathsf{Na}_3\mathsf{Zr}_2\mathsf{F}_{11}$ is not stable at the liquidus temperature.
In 47 to 42 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ compositions, the solid solution $\mathsf{Na}_9\mathsf{Zr}_8\mathsf{F}_{41}$ is the primary phase.
The liquidus temperatures of compositions with 47, 45, 43, and 42 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ are about 522, 520, 517, and $508^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively.
In 39 and 40 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$
compositions, the primary phase is $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ .

The liquidus temperature for the 39 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF}_{4}$ composition is above $513^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and for the 40 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF}_{4}$ composition it is near $510^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

For the 41 mole $\%$ composition the liquidus temperature is about $502^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and this composition is near a eutectic between $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ and the $\mathrm{Na}_{9}\mathrm{Zr}_{8}\mathrm{F}_{41}$ solid solution.
Below about $488^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{Zr}_{2}\mathrm{F}_{11}$ appears as fine-grained fibers.
Slowly cooled compositions with about 40 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF}_{4}$ usually undergo a reaction just after complete solidification that produces a fluff of very fine-grained material.
According to x-ray diffraction results, this material is a mixture of $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{Zr}_{2}\mathrm{F}_{11}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_{9}\mathrm{Zr}_{8}\mathrm{F}_{41}$ .
It is likely that the reaction is the solid-state conversion at about $488^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ of the eutectic into these compounds.
The formation of $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{Zr}_{2}\mathrm{F}_{11}$ in the solid state explains why it has always been found as fine grains.

Data previously reported4,5 indicated the existence of one eutectic and one compound in the NaF-UF3 system.
When a mixture containing 33 mole $\%$ UF3 and 67 mole $\%$ NaF was prepared by fusing NaF with previously prepared UF3, thermal effects were noted at 750, 695, 650, and $595^{\circ}C$ .
The only compound which has been observed (either Rb $_2$ UF $_5$ or Rb $_3$ UF $_6$ ) is described as a brown-orange isotropic phase with a refractive index of 1.44.
The 33.3 mole % UF $_3$ composition showed a thermal effect at $860^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .
A KF-LiF-UF $_3$ (48-48-4 mole %) mixture was prepared by reducing a previously fused KF-LiF-UF $_4$ mixture with 100% excess uranium metal.
It is probable, although not yet established, that the thermal effect at $558^{\circ}C$ represents the true liquidus temperature for this composition.
The fused melt contained about $15\%$ of an orange, low-birefringent phase of refractive index 1.43 and colorless isotropic materials with the refractive indices of KF and LiF.
A RbF-LiF-UF<sub>3</sub> (56-40-4 mole %) mixture was prepared by mixing uranium metal and previously prepared UF<sub>3</sub> with the fused alkali fluorides.
No thermal effect was observed above 475°C, the melting point of the RbF-LiF eutectic.

Since the filtration experiments reported above indicate that the liquidus temperature for a similar composition is above 550°C, it is concluded that the first separation of a UF<sub>3</sub>-containing phase from ternary mixtures in this region cannot be readily detected by conventional thermal-analysis techniques.
Petrographic examination revealed that the melt contained a colorless birefringent phase, presumably a LiF-RbF complex, and a brownish isotropic phase that was probably a RbF-UF<sub>3</sub> complex.

When the capsules had been heated to maximum temperatures of about $900^{\circ}C$ they were inverted so that the contents would mix.
The data obtained are presented in Table 4.3.
Two phases were observed in the unreduced mixture, the $\mathrm{Na}_{9}(\mathrm{Zr},\mathrm{U})_{8}\mathrm{F}_{41}$ solid solution and a small amount of colorless $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ .
Four phases were observed in the reduced mixtures: $\mathrm{UF}_{3}$ , a yellow isotropic material, a yellow-green solid solution, and $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ .

With increasing degrees of reduction, the solid solution became increasingly yellow, and the isotropic yellow phase, free $\mathrm{UF}_{3}$ , and yellow-green $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ appeared in increasing amounts.

This crystalline form of $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ , usually referred to as "normal" $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ , does not, according to present knowledge, dissolve tetravalent uranium.

The increasing demands for purified fluorides necessitated, early in this calendar year, an increased output of $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ from the production plant operated by Y-12 personnel.

The quality of the $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ was, accordingly, reduced; the iron content of the $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ increased from a mean of 250 to about 1000 ppm, while the nickel content rose from about 50 to about 200 ppm.

Although it is possible to prepare fluoride mixtures to rigorous specifications from this $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ , use of this material has reduced the efficiency of the production facility to a level considerably below that obtained during 1953 when the ARE fuel solvent was being processed.

Since the amount of iron to be stripped (by reduction of $\mathsf{FeF}_2$ with hydrogen) is much larger than that previously considered normal, the time required for the hydrogen stripping has increased from about 30 to nearly 100 hr, and hydrogen requirements have risen from 10,000 liters to nearly 25,000 liters per 250-lb batch of fluoride.

Two possible explanations for the increased resistance to mass transfer of the 400-series stainless steels in liquid lead have been considered.
It was thought possible that the oxide formed on the 400-series stainless steels possessed a structure which made it especially resistant to attack by liquid lead.
It has been demonstrated that relatively thick oxide films (approximately 1000 Å) greatly reduced mass transfer in loops containing types 304 and 347 stainless steel specimens.
An attempt was also made to remove completely the oxide film from type 446 stainless steel specimens mounted in a loop.

A small quartz tube was sealed into the loop at the bottom of the bend in the cold leg and, before the loop was loaded, the specimens were heated to $950^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ while hydrogen, which had been dried over magnesium perchlorate, was allowed to flow through the loop.
After 1 hr of this treatment the side arm was sealed off with a hand torch, and the loop was immediately loaded with liquid lead.
The loop plugged after 768 hr of operation with hot- and cold-leg temperatures of 805 and $525^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The long time required for the plugging of this loop was taken as an indication that the presence of an oxide film on the specimens was not the correct explanation for the relatively high resistance to mass transfer in liquid lead exhibited by type 446 stainless steel.

In such an event, the results of this test would be inconclusive; however, it is believed that the corrosion characteristics of type 446 stainless steel cannot be explained on the basis of the influence of thin oxide films on the surface of the test specimens.
The second approach made to the problem involved the assumption that a thin layer of sigma phase formed near the surface of the types 446 and
410 stainless steel hot-leg specimens and served as a barrier to solution of the specimens.

The presence of such a layer has not yet been revealed either by metallographic examination of the test specimens or by an x-ray study of filings taken from the surface of a type 446 stainless steel hot-leg specimen.
